did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture, specifically MobileNetV3Large with transfer learning, to classify skin cancer (benign vs. malignant) using the ISIC 2020 dataset. It also employed mixed precision training to reduce training time.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation of a MobileNetV3Large CNN with mixed precision training on the ISIC 2020 dataset. It details specific methods (black-hat morphological transformation, oversampling), reports new quantitative results (98% accuracy, 1,646 seconds training time reduction), and compares model performance, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Reducing Training Time in Skin Cancer Classification Using Convolutional Neural Network with Mixed Precision Implementation
journal: Jurnal RESTI (Rekayasa Sistem dan Teknologi Informasi)
brief_summary: This study investigates the use of the MobileNetV3Large convolutional neural network architecture with transfer learning to classify skin cancer as benign or malignant using the ISIC 2020 dataset. To address data imbalance and noise, the researchers applied black-hat morphological transformation and oversampling techniques to the dataset. The primary objective was to evaluate the impact of mixed precision training on model performance and training efficiency compared to standard precision training. The model without mixed precision achieved superior classification metrics, including 98% accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, while both models achieved an AUC-ROC of 1. Notably, the implementation of mixed precision training significantly reduced the training time by 1,646 seconds, representing an 8.39% speed increase. These findings suggest that mixed precision can meaningfully accelerate model training while maintaining competitive performance, which is beneficial for resource-constrained clinical applications.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies skin cancer into two main classes: benign and malignant. The benign class in the ISIC 2020 dataset includes specific disease types such as nevus, seborrheic keratosis, lentigo NOS, lichenoid keratosis, solar lentigo, cafe-au-lait macule, atypical melanocytic proliferation, and unknown.
The study classifies skin cancer into two main classes: benign and malignant. The malignant class in the ISIC 2020 dataset includes melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer into benign and malignant classes
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the aim is to classify skin cancer into two classes: benign and malignant using the ISIC 2020 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant) rather than continuous values. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2020 dataset, sourced from various institutions including Hospital Clinic de Barcelona, Medical University of Vienna, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Melanoma Institute Australia, University of Queensland, and University of Athens Medical School. It is an open-source dataset containing digital images for diagnosing skin diseases.

use.training: Used for training the MobileNetV3Large model. The dataset was split into 90% training and 10% validation. Oversampling was applied to the malignant class to balance it with the benign class (resulting in 32,542 images per class for training). Image augmentation (rotation, shift, shear, zoom, brightness, channel shift) was applied to the training data.
use.validation: Used for validation. The dataset was split into 90% training and 10% validation (6,508 images total: 3,254 benign, 3,254 malignant after oversampling).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared model performance using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC-ROC) and absolute training times, noting that mixed precision 'significantly reduced' training time by 1,646 seconds, but this appears to be a descriptive comparison rather than a statistical significance test.

ISIC 2020.notes: The model without mixed precision achieved superior performance metrics (98% across accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score) compared to the mixed precision model (97% across the same metrics). Both models achieved an AUC-ROC of 1.0. Mixed precision training reduced the total training time by 1,646 seconds (an 8.39% speed increase). The study notes that the slight decrease in accuracy for the mixed precision model is a trade-off for increased computational efficiency.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning (pre-trained weights implied by use of transfer learning and setting include_top=False)
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224x3 (after resizing dataset images to 256x256, input_shape set to (224, 224, 3) for MobileNetV3Large)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a uniform size of 256x256 pixels
Black-hat morphological transformation to remove obstructing objects (hair/noise) and enhance lesion boundaries
Oversampling (augmentation) of the malignant class to balance class distribution
Image augmentation techniques including rotation (range 25°), width shift (0.1), height shift (0.1), shear (0.2), zoom (0.2), brightness adjustment (0.5 to 1.5), and channel shift (0.05)
Nearest fill mode for handling new pixels after augmentation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the malignant class to balance it with the benign class, augmenting the malignant class to 32,542 images.
Rotation range of 25 degrees applied to training images.
Width shift range of 0.1 applied to training images.
Height shift range of 0.1 applied to training images.
Shear range of 0.2 applied to training images.
Zoom range of 0.2 applied to training images.
Brightness range from 0.5 to 1.5 applied to training images.
Channel shift range of 0.05 applied to training images.
Nearest fill mode used to fill in new pixels after augmentation.

conclusions: The MobileNetV3Large convolutional neural network model with transfer learning and standard (non-mixed) precision outperforms the mixed precision model in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, achieving 98% across these metrics.
The model utilizing mixed precision training achieved slightly lower performance metrics (97% for accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score) compared to the standard precision model.
Both the standard precision and mixed precision models achieved an AUC-ROC score of 1.0, indicating excellent performance in differentiating between benign and malignant skin lesions.
Mixed precision training significantly reduced the total training time by 1,646 seconds (an 8.39% speed increase), reducing the time from 19,603 seconds to 17,957 seconds.

The implementation of mixed precision involves a trade-off where the reduction in computational time and memory footprint comes at the expense of a slight decrease in classification accuracy due to rounding errors in lower precision arithmetic.

The study suggests that mixed precision training is a viable optimization technique for accelerating model training in resource-constrained environments, making it suitable for real-time clinical applications where computational efficiency is prioritized alongside competitive performance.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2020 dataset, which introduces potential dataset bias as the data is sourced from specific institutions (e.g., Hospital Clinic de Barcelona, Melanoma Institute Australia) and may not represent global dermatological diversity.
The paper lacks external validation; results are derived solely from a single internal 90/10 train-validation split, limiting the generalizability of the findings to independent clinical populations.

Formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was not performed; the claim that mixed precision 'significantly reduced' training time is based on descriptive absolute differences (1,646 seconds) rather than statistical significance.

The model achieved perfect AUC-ROC scores of 1.0 for both standard and mixed precision variants, which is unusually high for skin cancer classification tasks and may indicate data leakage, overly optimistic validation splits, or lack of sufficient noise/complexity in the test set.

The study does not report a separate testing phase or confusion matrix details for the final evaluation, relying instead on validation metrics which may overestimate performance compared to a held-out test set.

The primary contribution is computational efficiency (8.39% training time reduction) rather than diagnostic accuracy, as the standard precision model actually outperformed the mixed precision model in accuracy (98% vs 97%).

The hardware environment (Google Colab T4 GPU) is not standardized for clinical deployment, and the reported training times are specific to this cloud infrastructure, limiting direct comparison with other studies using different hardware.
The paper does not address critical clinical deployment concerns such as inference latency, model interpretability, or integration with existing dermatological workflows.
